comments.title: I still need magical
comments.content:
I am still having trouble with a terrible neighbor whom I’ve complained of before. Due to circumstance, it seems that magic is currently my only option for dealing with her *exceedingly* persistent harassment. I’m desperate for good effective advice.

I can’t afford to move and I can’t afford to sue, so those aren’t options. Nor can I afford the surveillance cams that would be needed to bust her. I’ve tried city hall and they’ve proven worthless. The police also are useless for this; I’ve asked. Complaining to the landlords would almost certainly be worse than useless, putting at hazard our already precarious housing situation.

All that is necessary to provoke one or more episodes of harassment from this individual is for me to be present on some portion of the rented property, where I have a right to be, and minding my own business, reading a book or the like, while she also is in some nearby portion of the property. This happened in the past couple of days, provoking an incident. I know from prior experience that she is likely to do still more nasty deeds soon, perhaps tonight.

Things she is likely to do, based on past history:

Pour poison on the soil over the roots of the rose bushes that I planted in the back yard for my mother, causing them to wither (two occasions at least, probably more). We could smell the stench from the rotting roots and see the wilting & weakening of the plants.

Just as the rose buds are getting ready to bloom, snipping off and so destroying them (two or three occasions). She first did this just a couple of days before Mother’s Day, when nine blossoms were about to open.

Persistently trample other flowers we have been cultivating in the same area (destroyed a robust patch of poppies in this way, as well as other flowering plants).

Place a large sample of fresh cat excrement on our front or back porch (twelve to fifteen occasions).

Place small bits of old dried excrement on our stairs, front or back porch (many occasions)

Place cat excrement in our portion of the back yard (many occasions, and sometimes in huge quantities, saved up over time; I discovered such a pile today). On one occasion, it was a very large fresh pile of dog excrement.

Place other unsightly objects in our portion of the back yard, or on our stairs or porch - broken glass or crockery, rubbish, etc.

Bang loudly on the floor under my bed in the middle of the night, waking me up (several occasions).

Persistent verbal harassment against me. This was so bad that, through the help of friends, I managed to get a lawyer to send her a cease-and desist letter. This stopped her, mostly, but the harassment can resume at any time.

The original provocation? She was running a laundry business illegally out of her basement apartment, keeping us up all night, and we tried to shut her down. This is when we learned that city hall won’t do diddly.

This person is from Hong Kong and appears to be in her early 70s. I suppose some of her tricks might be forms of folk magic from that part of the world. She likes to feed stray cats, which explains her access to cat poop. The food she leaves out for them attracts many pests.

I’ve tried hotfoot powder and it didn’t work (maybe I didn’t do it right?). I’ve tried a couple of magical attacks, with severe consequences - to myself.

The only other option I can imagine would be to contact her adult daughter, who appears to be a rational person. I doubt the lady has told her daughter of these her deeds. The daughter might be able to persuade her to lay off. But I only have one address for the two of them, so there’s no contacting just one; and anyway, how do I know this won’t hurt rather than help my situation? It seems to me that I need powerful, ethical, effective magic. If you have any suggestions, I’m willing to do what it takes, even if it involves considerable effort and inconvenience.

replies.title: Re: I still need magical
replies.content:
I have a very straightforward suggestion. Do a series of magical workings to get the money you need to move, and follow up the workings by engaging in practical measures (such as working extra hours) to do the same thing. Magic is not a cure-all, especially with this kind of tangled interpersonal situation; what's more, the old woman may know more about magic than you do.

By the way, um, magical attacks? Bad idea. Really bad idea. You may have locked yourself into a bad situation by doing that.

comments.title: An experence of living nature
comments.content:
Hi JMG

I have an experience to share and I’d like to hear what other people have experienced along the same lines.

I was on a walk beside a river among some trees trying to connect to the plants around me. I’m in the southern hemisphere so it’s approaching spring now. Some of the willows beside the river were starting to put out their first leaves and there were a few trees covered in flowers. I perceived a feeling like stretching after waking up from sleep from the trees putting out their leaves. It felt like a tree covered in flowers was singing. Some pine trees felt like they were grumbling “grow grow grow...”. it felt like the grass plants were squabbling amongst themselves. I got the impression that if the plants were left to their own devices it would be a complete mess. It felt like there were other spiritual beings which acted to balance out the ecosystem and bring about the complex and dynamic beauty which we see in functioning ecosystems. I also got an impression from the goddess Elen who I was trying to talk to at the time.
I’d like to hear it this matches up with the experiences of others and what experiences they’ve had in nature.

Thank you JMG for providing this forum where we can talk about this kind of thing openly.

replies.title: Re: An experence of living nature
replies.content: This is pretty much what I experience whenever I'm outdoors in spring and turn my attention to the inner side of things. A great many of the old occult authors -- I'm thinking here especially of Geoffrey Hodson and Rudolf Steiner -- have very similar things to say, as indeed do the Findhorn books. So you're in very good company!

replies.title: Re: An experence of living nature
replies.content: Do you have any advice other than practice for strengthening this form of perception? Does the course in the DMH help this?

replies.title: Re: An experence of living nature
replies.content: It's best to allow it to ripen naturally. The best thing I know to help it do that is daily meditation using the method of discursive meditation taught in The Druidry Handbook and (more briefly) in The Druid Magic Handbook.

Also, for a few months I had a sort of Guru, named White Flame. He appeared to speak the Green language in real time two(or more!)-way communication, I was awestruck, he said "When you kill the Ego, you Know Everything" I believed him and begged for teaching. I also had at that time a practice of daily major arcane tarot divination (1 card automatically emailed to me daily)from which I would take my magickal weather/Karmic Lesson-y thing. So I need to find out how to get back on the horse so to speak...any cures for magickal ptsd?

replies.title: Re: Room Cleaning, Dragon Slaying, and other Apollonian Pursuits.
replies.content:
1) Have you tried journaling? Writing out a series of conversations between yourself and your feelings is a good way to unpack the childhood experiences that are usually involved in feelings of the kind you describe. It's important not to try to use magic to blot out those feelings -- all you'll do is suppress them, and they'll pop back up again, usually in an even nastier form.

The very short form is that most repetitive emotional problems happen because you had to deal with very strong unwelcome emotions when you were too young to do it by yourself, and didn't get the help you needed from others -- so you bottled the emotions up. They're still there, and they come boiling up whenever you enter into a situation that reminds you of the scene that caused the original emotions. Some psychotherapists are good at helping people encounter and release childhood emotional messes, but these days, most aren't, so journaling is often an effective tool to manage the encounter.

2) I think most people who get into occultism have at least one Bad Teacher Experience(tm); I was fortunate in that mine was with a martial arts and Zen macrobiotics teacher, and I came through it with nothing worse than mild malnutrition. You'll be fine, and now you know something about bad teachers. One basic rule: when somebody insists you have to "kill the Ego," watch them to make sure they've done it. If they haven't -- and most people who use this phrase haven't -- what they want you to do is kill your ego so theirs can feed on it, and on you.

3) Take it easy for a while. Regular tarot readings aren't going to do you any harm; daily prayer to Apollo will do a lot of good -- he's a healing god among other things, thus well suited to your present situation -- and a basic banishing ritual such as the one you linked to is a good thing to do regularly, as well. Another practice that helps is to spend at least fifteen minutes a week in a natural environment, not thinking or talking, just paying attention to what you experience through your physical senses -- leave the clairvoyance for later; your goal at this stage is to ground yourself in physical reality. After a few months of this, and a lot of journaling, you should be ready to take up regular magical practice if that's what calls you.

comments.title: Summoning and Banishing in CGD
comments.content:
Hi John,

What am I summoning and banishing in the LRP Celtic Golden Dawn? I assumed it was the four gods, but I feel that it is more than that. How much do I need to know of what’s around? Or do I trust summoning to bring good things and vice versa?

Thanks for your guidance

replies.title: Re: Summoning and Banishing in CGD
replies.content: No, it's not the four gods -- neither you nor any other human being has the power to summon or banish a god! "Summoning" and "banishing" are shorthand terms for a somewhat complex process. A summoning ritual brings you into closer contact with the spiritual world; a banishing ritual brings you back into closer contact with the material world. In both cases you call on gods and use a symbol of balanced power to make the transition in a balanced and protected fashion.

comments.title: of Saints and Avatar
comments.content:
About 20 years ago when I was in the thick of my eastern meditation practice I had a trip to India.
A couple of the interesting things that happened;
1. I came across the Karmapa Lama (the one born in China and who escaped). He was about 21 at the time and his energy was phenomenal! So much way more spiritual strength/power than anything I had experienced before or since.
I could feel his presence in the hallway outside the room while waiting for him.
The only reasonable explanation I can come up with for a 21 year old to have that spiritual power is for him to be an Avatar.
(talking to others back at the ashram, they'd met the other Karmapa Lama and apparently the other is just as strong)
Question, Have you met or aware of any avatars in the western tradition? And who are they?

2. By luck one day we met and followed a Hindu priest on his daily rounds (I was travelling with followers of the same lineage as him). At some point in the day we were introduced to a saint, his energy was significant (but not too strong), but exceptionally clean. This makes sense to me as a good way to define a saint.
Are there real saints like him with this exceptionally clean energy in the western tradition? (excluding those called saints by a church for what I assume is more political than spiritual reasons)

-KNS

replies.title: Re: of Saints and Avatar
replies.content:
1) The process of repeated reincarnation is something that's been developed over centuries in Tibet. It's not something you find in most other Buddhist countries, even in places where esoteric Buddhism is practiced. It's also not something that Western spiritual traditions have done, as far as I know, so no, I don't know of any.

2) Every tradition has its saints. I've met elderly Christian ladies who have no religious function other than member of a congregation, who radiate clear light so strong I practically had to blink looking at them. (One of these days I'll do a post passing on the advice about daily prayer and Bible study I got from one of them -- if you happen to belong to any religion that has a sacred scripture, it's first-rate stuff.) So, yes, there are saints of that sort all through Western spiritual traditions; I've never encountered one in a leadership or clergy position in a religious organization, but they do exist.

replies.title: Re: of Saints and Avatar
replies.content: Very interesting! I would be very interested in hearing about this prayer and scripture study. :-)

replies.title: Re: of Saints and Avatar
replies.content:
Here's the simple version.

Every morning, set aside 15 or 20 minutes first thing, before breakfast, and if all possible right after waking. Begin by reading one chapter out of the Bible. (The woman who taught me this insisted that you should never begin with Genesis, but should start with Chapter 1 of the Gospel of Matthew and go through the whole New Testament before going onto the Old Testament. Most people who try to read the Bible daily and start with Genesis, she said, bog down in "the begats" and never get any further.)

So you read one chapter, and then you think about what you've read, turn it over in your mind, see how well you understand it, see what questions it raises in your mind. Then you pray to Christ for the grace of the Holy Spirit to help you understand what you've read, and finish by repeating the Lord's Prayer. Then you go about your day. That 15 or 20 minutes daily, combined with Sundays at church, gave her an extraordinarily rich spiritual life and the brightest and cleanest aura I think I've ever encountered.

comments.title: Spirit below gate
comments.content:
(from Myriam)

I have been moving at a glacial pace in my progress because of work load and other commitments, though it has been sort of steady, with some breaks when too exhausted or ill. Two weeks ago or so, I got to the spirit below in the SOP. A couple of observations:

It may sound strange, but I recognized this force. I know this force from before. It is eager to connect. It is already in me, and has always been.

It also seems to me to be the same force that flows through the first four gates, but the clear, raw source, not the flavour, if that makes sense.

And it is incredibly powerful. When I began doing that step, the force would render me so weak I could barely stand, and felt ill. I'm sometimes doubled over with weakness. Once, it crushed me so much I couldn't stand and had to go lie down. So I left the circle, lay down for a few minutes, then thought it a bad idea to leave the circle, so went back and finished a very weak and wobbly circulation of the lights.

It does seem to be getting better. I am careful to have eaten not too long before, and don't do the SOP first thing in the morning when my blood sugars are low, nor late at night which I've realized has been causing all the insomnia I've had in the past (thanks to a comment you made a couple weeks ago). I'm able to stand straight to finish that gate, but the strength of the force is still there.

A couple of questions:

Any thoughts on this? Is this normal? Something I'm not doing right?

I'm wondering if the weakness has anything to do with the breathing I'm doing during the SOP. I prepare myself before I start with 9 relaxing deep breaths, just focusing on the breathing of the solar plexus, and then during the SOP itself at each gate, after I imagine the symbols appropriate to each gate (early morning air, full sun and fire, the St-Lawrence river which I live beside, and a forest) I breathe deeply 3 times imagining the colour of each gate flowing into my solar plexus and filling me. Is it possible by the time I get to the spirit below gate that I'm weak because of hyperventilation? That wouldn't explain the force of that gate, though.

Was there any danger in leaving the circle before I finished the circulation of the lights?

Is it the same force at its source as the other gates? Or are they supposed to be different forces?

Why is that force so much more powerful (to me anyway) than the other gates? Are all the spirit gates more powerful than the first four gates?

I'm thinking of taking my time with this gate, and not doing the next one until I no longer feel the weakness. Would you advise this? Or would it be helpful to balance it with spirit above asap? I noticed that it was a very good thing that you said to learn the gates first with air then water before doing fire. The balancing of air with water and fire with earth as I was learning felt necessary. Is the same thing happening here?

I really appreciate the time you take to share your thoughts on all this! And for the opportunity to ask.

replies.title: Re: Spirit below gate
replies.content:
1) You probably have had some kind of connection with the telluric current via spiritual or magical work done in a past incarnation. When you get a disproportionate response to something like this, that's very often what's involved. No, it's not hyperventilation.

replies.title: Re: Rapid change of states
replies.content:
That's something you see fairly often in people who convince themselves that the details don't matter, or that the universe is set up to give them whatever they want, or what have you. (We're seeing a world-class example of it here in the US, courtesy of Elon Musk, for whom the details are starting to matter in a very big way.) The methods of positive thinking can shield you from the consequences of your actions for a while, but that just means that the consequences build up a head of pressure, and sooner or later the barrier cracks and down you go.

replies.title: ordering of planes
replies.content:
On your second question, I have no meditations to offer but I can propose alternate vocabulary.

Some people find it helpful to describe the relationships of levels, planes, emanations and other schemata which are influenced by Neoplatonism as deeper/less deep or closer/farther (with reference to the origin or ultimate source). Some occult texts refer to some levels as more or less subtle or of higher/lower vibration, but these are metaphors that imply a ranking in my mind.

Changing the adjectives will not permanently stop the habit of rating levels or emanations as better/worse. I find it helps to think of or remind myself that these emanations or worlds are continuously arising from and returning to the Source, and that they interpenetrate each other.

In some cosmologies, there is an axis of more differentiated/more unified, which seems to me more neutral than higher/lower, and also ties in with current understanding of evolutionary biology.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Okay, "the planes" in an author such as Knight is always a reference to the Theosophical system of seven planes, each of which is divided into seven sub-planes. (Dion Fortune had Theosophical training, so she and her students tended to use that language pretty consistently.) A look at a basic textbook such as William Q. Judge's The Ocean of Theosophy (which you can find online here will give you a good sense of the tradition he's working in.

With regard to the value judgments applied to "higher" and "lower," you'll simply need to try to catch yourself when you make that judgment, and remind yourself that every Sphere is equally holy. That in itself is a worthwhile exercise, and good for training the will as well.

comments.title: Alienation
comments.content:
A few months ago I brought up the need to keep secrets vis-a-vis my spiritual practices and the world. As a follow up, I can say that this has proven to be easier than I would have thought. The issue though, is with a sense of acutely felt alienation between myself and others.

Basically, spiritual practices have amped up my 'voltage,' for lack of a better term. I can think more coherently, feel more compassionately, and my dream life is filled with sunlight and symbols. My aura is light and clean. This is great!

What is painful, acutely so, is the sense, real or imagined, is the alienation. Of course, a lot of this alienation is built into the environment, but some of it is a chasm that has opened between myself and others. Frankly, and I am half-ashamed to admit this, discourse with most people reminds me much more of the barking of dogs more than conversing with self-aware beings. This is an eerie experience, honestly. It is like the stories of talking animals in folklore, but reversed, with barking, mewing or cawing humans. It seems too, that people are a bit scared of me too, since this voltage can be uncanny, it is bright and intimate, and can involve things like telepathy and the like.

I think I'm describing an experience that others must have too. The experience that with spiritual practices that move one towards the grand ideal ofipsississimus, that one is going to have to traverse a landscape where people seem, relatively less developed, or actively deluded or crazed. I don't personally feel more special or better, just different and distinct, btw, but it is a very lonely sort of difference. Of course, everyone is distinct and weird in their own ways, but not always in manners that cause acute alienation to bear down on their relationships with others. Perhaps I'm being myopic and inconsiderate in this...can't tell.

To rectify this I've started volunteering at a soup kitchen to get me around more people in meaningful activity, and so far so good! I plan on moving from the half-embalmed suburb where I live too sometime in the next year, which is something too. I'm curious though JMG, how you emotionally navigate this sense of difference, of loneliness, of alienation? Are there general thoughts you think may have bearing on this sort of experience?

replies.title: Re: Alienation
replies.content:
I'm probably not the best person to ask about this, because solitude is kind of my preferred state -- dealing with people was so difficult in my childhood, due partly to my Aspergers syndrome and partly to the way that most people reacted to my Aspergers syndrome, that being alone with a book or what have you became my comfortable place early on, and dealing with people even now is something I have to nerve myself up for.

But the experience you're having is extremely common. There's a good reason Manly P. Hall titled one of his books on occultism The Ways of the Lonely Ones. The thing that seems to work is to find people to associate with who are also actively involved in spiritual practices of one kind or another. Those are the people you'll find you can communicate with.

replies.title: Re: Alienation

replies.title: Re: Alienation
replies.content:
(sgage here)

Hello Violet,

Yes, alienation, on so many levels. The fact of the matter is that there is indeed a sort of gap between you (and me) and the average citizen. It can be painful, and I've been dealing with this for years - well, decades.

Two things have helped me cope. One, find a more-or-less like-minded person if you can, so you don't feel quite so utterly alone. I have usually had one person in my life that I could talk to about things really important and basic. This is not necessarily someone that I'm in a "relationship" with, usually isn't, in fact. Of course, JMG's blogs can help enormously, but it's not the same as a personal thing. (Wasn't the potluck in E.Prov. wonderful!)

The other thing is Love. Everyone is doing their thing. In these times, that often means trying to make it through another day/week/month. People in this state are often not very open to change of any kind, or even hope. They are just keeping their head down, and they are exhausted on many levels. I perceive that people are really hurting, and as a rather empathetic person, I feel it hard. I will not and/or can not shut down my feelings in this regard. The only recourse that I've found to help at all is to love them. I try very hard to treat everyone I have dealings with as a fellow traveller, even if they're stuck somewhere that seems so alien and dead-endish to me. I talk with them, commiserate with them, try hard to coax a laugh out of 'em and laugh with them... I think the soup kitchen thing is exactly the right idea.

Just do YOUR thing, and let your light shine - everybody wins! This gap, this alienation - yes, it's real, but maybe also somewhat illusory? I don't know. One does one's best...

I don't maybe have the distinction of a clean bright aura, I don't know what auras look like, and mine I imagine most likely looks like its made of plywood and static electricity, or some such daft thing.

A few days ago I was talking with a friend, who is a better thinker than you are likely to meet in any given year, and we were on about some shadow projection we observed in a mutual acquaintance. Then I pointed out a mirror image the shadow my friend was commenting on. Their jaw locked, face pale, eyes fixed. Animal reaction, sound of predator. I had equated my friend's shadow with the shadow of our acquaintance. Suddenly we go from a close grove, to a distant planet. This was a useful moment because it was uncomfortable for me in a way which taught me a little bit about my own shadow. But, I saw something that one isn't suppose to see.

They make good money, but are actually poor for some weird reason, the scraps from my garden provided a far above par feast to them. One person I didn't know well, and I could feel my self almost oppressing them just because they were unaccustomed to being around a person loudly outside of their bubble. I don't yet know if that was a productive meeting or not.

I know Nietzsche put in the words of Zarathustra.
"Seriously guys, I walk by people like passing the limbs and parts of human beings!
It's really messed up, that I find man broken up, and scattered about, like going through combat zone or slaughter house.
And looking back, back to the past, it's always like that: chunks and limbs and fearful chances—but not a whole person!
Today, same as it always be! My friends—that's what messes with ME."

42 on redemption, paraphrased.

More and more I am a clown. You know clowning is a serious business spiritual path. Acting out being the rube the outsider, all the while winking that you know how the word games of the insiders are played and winking. The thing I can say of it comes to this, that we are all different and therefore alone, but if you try to hone what is individual to you, then you will make that fact glaring. For those who still relish togetherness with others, even those who are strange and yapping, sometimes it is good to speak yap with them. After all one doesn't speak finnish to the bantu. Playful and loving teasing is the most pleasant thing sometimes. But, me I have always acted a part in social settings, because deep down, with the mask off, I am dreadfully shy.

Ray Wharton

The Cosmic Doctrine would suggest "releasing the Wendigo" as the basic strategy. comments.title: Very first magic step
comments.content:
Happy Monday, Mr. Greer. I have found a great deal of good sense in your writings, both sociopolitical and esoteric, since I discovered them a couple of years ago. Perhaps you have helped others through dilemmas similar to mine.

First, to saddle you with yet another reader biography. I shall strive to be pertinent. I have always been abstractly pagan in personal philosophy, and I have reached a stage in my life where I want and need an active spiritual practice. I believe in magic, as you have described it, and I have no qualms with others who practice it.

Unfortunately, I have profound difficulties with conceiving of myself as a potentially magical person. Discussions with my counselor suggest that is because when I say "magical" I primarily mean "capable of self-determination." And it is true that I do not really consider myself such a person... but I would like to be. And it does seem like an issue that magic could help with.

But how do I even begin effectively? I have attempted a couple of simple rituals over the years and felt nothing but awkward and uneasy after. What can I do to get myself past this mental block and start properly on the long path to becoming an effective magician? My counselor is sympathetic and encouraging, but unfortunately is not well-versed enough in the matter of esoteric practices to offer specific advice.

Cordially, Tif

replies.title: Re: Very first magic step
replies.content:
One of the classic ways to deal with this sort of thing is the old New Thought practice of affirmation. Simply start saying to yourself, several times a day, "I am capable of self-determination." The first few times you do this, or the first few hundred, you'll probably get an internal tantrum in response. Ignore it, and keep on repeating it.

(It's important, by the way, to use a present tense declarative sentence like the one I've given. Your mind listens to those.)

Along with this, get yourself a notebook or blank book of some kind, and spend fifteen minutes or so a day journaling. Write "I am capable of self-determination" at the top of the first sheet of paper and then, without self-censoring, write down everything that comes into your head in response to that sentence. Keep doing this day by day until you've gottento all the miserable childhood experiences whose bottled-up emotions are surfacing when you try to think of yourself as capable of self-determination.

One other thing. You already are a magical person. Everybody is always practicing magic, day in and day out. What makes somebody an operative mage isn't that they have powers nobody else has; it's that they use their magical powers consciously, to get the lives they want, rather than using them unconsciously, to get whatever life they were programmed to get by their parents, their culture, and their childhood experiences. You already have all the power you will ever need; you just have to wake up to that fact, stop using your powers to frustrate yourself, and start using them to live your dreams.

replies.title: Re: Very first magic step
replies.content:
That is an encouraging way to consider things, and one that (obviously) had not occurred to me. Thank you for articulating it. I will expand my current journaling practice along the line suggested. I had always dismissed affirmations as a rah-rah, new-agey sort of thing, but since you endorse the practice, I'll give it the benefit of the doubt. Thank you!
Tif

replies.title: Re: Very first magic step
replies.content: Oh, it can be done in a rah-rah new-agey kind of way, no question, but the New Age (and New Thought before it) have some things worth learning, and if you need to mess with childhood programming, affirmation is a great tool. In my experience, it needs to be balanced by something like journaling, so that you don't just cover over old emotional wounds or blind yourself to unnoticed realities and unmet needs, but the practice itself works.

comments.title: linking to the land through story and ceremony
comments.content:
Hello,
Last Friday, August 17th, was exactly one year from the day I fell off my bicycle. In fact I recovered incredibly quickly from a broken rib and some internal bleeding the rib caused by injuring my kidney and spleen. I was back at work inside of two weeks and back on the bicycle,with full confidence, after about four months...

Anyway, there so happens to be a roadside well, belonging to neighbours, very close to the site where I came off my bike, and as I have passed the spot, often twice daily, I have begun to give a mental salute to the nameless "lady of the well". I have thanked her for bringing the help I needed in that moment, and also for my recovery. I decided I would, with the neighbour's permission, visit the well on the anniversary, and pay my respects in person.

I had prepared a wee ceremony that would include the offering of silver (well unknown metal jewellery, anyway) for the lady, and a drawing of water from the well, on which the lady's blessing would be asked, for purposes of consecrating* (or at least, making sacred) a different well, one that is closer to my house, and to which I have access without running a road traffic hazard.

Well, leaving work on Friday, with a wee box of chocolates for the neighbours, and in my pocket a brooch for the lady, and a wee glass container for water, I actually felt excited, as though I was going on a date. :)

Anyway, having visited both neighbour and well, and carried out my intentions, I am now working with my husband to develop the other well, which is the domestic well formerly used by his family at his old, now ruined, childhood cottage. It had become overgrown, and lost a stone or two, and needed a good clean. So we have confabulated some plans to bring it back to its glory.

My question (after all that) is: do you have any suggestions as to where to look for inspiration to prepare a ceremony for once the work is complete? We want to entrust the well over to the lady, let it be her habitation, and to establish it as a sacred place of the land? I understand that we need to be speaking to her about this already... And I am doing so. However, hearing the "small voice" from the other side of the conversation is something I'm practicing, but very far from perfect on...

* If I recall correctly, you have said a consecration should apply to something only you will touch, thereafter. Obviously a well does not fall in the category of things that only we will ever touch. Still, in "handing it up" to be sacred, we will also be handing ourselves up as caretakers. Whatever you call that.

replies.title: Re: linking to the land through story and ceremony
replies.content:
It's a different kind of consecration, of course -- much more like the sort of thing used with churches, temples, stone circles, and so on. I don't know of a good source for rituals of this kind, but you should be able to construct something fairly readily using an ordinary opening and closing ritual and an invocation of the Lady of the Well.

I know I don't have to tell you that what you're doing is profoundly traditional and taps into very deep energies. As for "caretakers of the sacred well," the old terms were, I believe, "priestess and priest."

replies.title: Re: Soul
replies.content: That's one of those really slippery words that I use only in writings for a general audience, since it's too vague to make a good technical term. The human being as a whole system is a complicated structure that exists on all the levels of being we're able to perceive. The classification I prefer to use notes the physical body, which is the thing that anatomists study and doctors treat; the etheric body, which is the body of life energy, the thing that acupuncturists work with; the astral body, which is the body of imagination and concrete consciousness; the mental body, which is the body of abstract consciousness, where understanding happens; and the spiritual body, the core of pure consciousness at the center of it all. The spiritual and mental body reincarnate; the physical and etheric body die; the astral body is complex, and its parts return to their sources in the astral world.

replies.title: Re: Soul
replies.content: Thank you

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: A lot depends on what you mean by "pseudoscientific." That phrase gets bandied about quite a bit these days! Dissident religious movements like Scientology have a normal life cycle; unless they manage to break out into the religious big time and find a place for themselves as enduring denominations, the way the Mormon Church did, they have a growth phase, a stabilization phase, a long slow decline, and then a descent into irrelevance or extinction. I figure the Church of Scientology is about a generation away from the irrelevance-or-extinction part of that story, so no, it won't be around when things bottom out one to three centuries from now.

comments.title: Choronzon and the watcher in the threshold
comments.content: I get that they are not exactly deities or spirits. What are they?

replies.title: Re: Choronzon and the watcher in the threshold
replies.content:
I tend to think that Choronzon was Crowley's term for what he saw after drinking one too many glasses of Old Overcoat on an empty stomach. Yes, I know, in Thelemic theory, he's supposed to be the Dweller in the Abyss, who destroys the ego of the initiate as the latter passes through the Abyss, but you know, nobody who actually knew Aleister Crowley seems to have believed for long that the Not-so-great Beast ever got around to destroying his own ego -- quite the contrary, in fact.

As for the Watcher on the Threshold, that's a convenient name for the terror of the unseen everyone has to face at some point in the process of taking up magical study. It's not a being, though (due to religious beliefs and the incurable human habit of personifying everything) it's sometimes experienced as one. I prefer to talk about the TSW moment: the point when it really hits home that, ahem, This Stuff Works -- yes, that's the polite version -- and the beginner generally has to be talked down from a pretty fair state of panic.

comments.title: The etheric vs. the physical
comments.content:
JMG-

Thank you for being kind enough to answer my question last week on the role of vital force relative to the physcal body; as tends to happen, though, the answer just raises new questions, since it seems I understand the magical cosmos less well than I might have hoped.

Last week you wrote, "All physical matter, whether or not it belongs to a living body, is shaped and directed by patterns of etheric force, which in turn are shaped and directed by patterns of higher levels of being." Am I correct to surmise that "patterns of etheric force" that guide physical matter, here, are synonymous with the "laws of physics" discussed in science classes? Does that imply that physicists (or chemists, cell biologists, etc) are unwittingly dealing with the etheric? (...And if that's the case, where's the boundary? Are car mechanics dealing with the etheric, too?)

In the same vein; if the physical world doesn't "stand by itself" and is the product of continuous etheric creation, why isn't magic more "supernaturally" powerful than it seems to be? I.e., why does it tend to "follow nature," as I recall you putting it. If the force of gravity pulling a stone to the ground is purely a result of etheric patterns, and etheric patterns are subject to the thoughts and will of magicians/gods/spirits/etc, what stops such a being from levitating a stone by altering the pattern of gravity? (Or what stops them from turning it colors by altering the etheric pattern of the stone itself, or vaporizing it by dissipating the pattern, or bringing it to life by infusing it with nwyfre, or other such things?)

Many thanks!

-A

replies.title: Re: The etheric vs. the physical
replies.content:
1) No, they're not quite synonymous. Remember that the "laws of physics" aren't actually out there in the world of phenomena; they're a set of mathematical models in the minds of certain human beings, which allow the observed phenomena of nature to be predicted more or less accurately. The patterns of etheric force can be directly experienced through the body -- most of us do this all the time, though our culture teaches us to ignore that set of perceptions. As for car mechanics, of course they're dealing with the etheric -- and so are you, every time you take a breath or reach for something with one of your hands.

2) A metaphor may help. You're standing by a waterfall, with a thousand gallons a second crashing over the brink and plunging down into the roiling waters below. You have an ordinary garden hose. Can you, by aiming the garden hose, make the waterfall flow uphill? Of course not. The mage is in a similar situation. The pattern of gravity that makes a stone fall to the ground and stay there is held in place by a massive flow of etheric force, which is itself held in place by other forces cascading down from the highest level of being. Your chance of changing that through magic is right up there with the garden hose being able to redirect the waterfall. As for the gods, er, where did you think the etheric patterns that make gravity come from?

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

I regularly read Ecosophia and the Archdruid Report prior to it. I really appreciate your writing, it has been the impetus for many thoughtful and stimulating conversations between my wife and I. So, many thanks for all the mentally stimulating work.

Some background to my question. I'm a pagan, not in any organized sense, just in the sense that I recognize that there is more to the existence than atheism accounts for and I'm not satisfied with the explanation that any conventional religions provide.

My wife and I do maintain an alter in our house, to our ancestors, we started celebrating pagan holidays (wheel of the year type holidays), occasionally we consult the runes (we use elder futhark runes), and we make offerings to the local land spirits/faeries.

I also do combatives training physical training, gun-fighting training, unarmed fighting etc. Occasionally, usually when doing some particularly hard physical training, my body will start screaming at me to stop and I'll motivate myself by thinking "I don't want to disappoint/ I want to impress my ancestors/Thor/Tyr".

I don't take religion all too seriously. I don't mean this in an "I don't believe it but I do it because..." sense. To the contrary, I do believe in the influence of our ancestors, I have long been able to "feel" the energy in certain places, and I've had a smattering of interactions with what i'd call "other" beings.

In other words, I treat all those mystical things around us with that same attitude that I generally treat other people. I recognize them and don't act disrespectfully and they go about their business while I go about mine.

Which brings me to my present situation.

The other day, I was working as a wildland firefighter. We were waiting for another crew to prepare to do a burnout (purposely light a fire to consume fuels between a constructed fireline and the actual fire). While sitting around waiting, enjoying the mountain scenery, I suddenly thought about writing a poem to my wife. This is pretty odd for me, in my entire life, I have written exactly 0 poems. I'm not a terribly artistic person, but I pulled out my notebook and started writing a poem. I really didn't even have trouble, the lines all came pretty easily and I composed my poem.

A little later, the other crew started firing the hill off above me. The fire on the hillside was picking up pretty well and the thought popped into my head "Baldr loves this". Ummm, who is Baldr? Sitting there without access to the internet I tried to wrack my brain, it sounds familiar but I don't know who that is. Thoughts kept popping into my head about Baldr for the rest of the day. That night I couldn't sleep and in the dark I pulled my notebook out and scribbled "who is Baldr?" on a blank page so that I would remember to look it up.

Flash forward a couple of days, we spent our last night before leaving the fire at a larger camp and I had e-mail, no internet, but I could read my messages. In my messages were notifications for posts on Ecosophia, so I read the last two posts I had missed, Kek Wars parts 3 and 4. The next day we left the camp and I was letting my mind wander a bit, thinking about Jung's archetypes.

I don't know if you are familiar with Gary Goldschneider's Secret Language of Destiny, but it provides "life paths" based on your birth date. In the book a significant part of my life path is realizing that other people project their own images on to me, often in a negative manner. I was reflecting on this, along with Jung's archetypes when it suddenly dawned on me how much people project images on to me both positive and negative. It was a bit of personal epiphany for me.

About the time this occurred to me I looked over and saw two ravens sitting on the chimney of an old house. That snapped my attention in to focus, they were in the perfect Huginn and Muninn pose (that iconic pose, facing each other one with head up and the other head down). That surprised me a bit and I texted my wife.

A little later she texted me back some picture of pages of a book on American Indian animal medicine. One of the interesting things to me about this was the repeated use of the term “the void”. Two weeks before I had been writing about discovering the nature of the void that was in me and how I have come to terms with it.

I however was still thinking of the two ravens in terms of Odin. It wouldn't get out of my head that the two ravens were a sign from Odin. This is a bit odd for me because, although I would say I'm spiritual, I didn't explicitly believe in any “gods”. I had never directly experienced anything that I would call a god and while I occasionally thought about Thor and Tyr, that was more in a symbolic way than a literal one. That being said, I have still always exercised a healthy caution where gods are concerned. Odin has always been a god that I have been careful not to commune with. This isn't out of any personal dislike or issue, more just that I have always viewed Odin as an extremely serious god, not to be taken lightly and with my laid back views of spirituality, probably not someone I'd want to inadvertently offend.

As soon as I got internet I looked up Huginn and Muninn, then I looked up Odin. Odin, it turns out, is the father of Baldr and is also the god of poetry and “the ability to compose poetry is a gift he grants at his pleasure.”

In one of your posts on the Kek wars, you mentioned the TSW moment. This was a similar moment for me. Odin is real, not in the allegorical symbolic way, in the he is an individual that exists and just communicated with me way. I was a bit unnerved by all this.

I was also struck by the account of Baldr, apparently an exceedingly happy god who was well liked by all (before being killed). I thought that was interesting because for a great deal of my life I have not been a very happy person, but over the last decade I have slowly worked my way in to a far more positive outlook on life. I generally go through life happy now and I love working as a firefighter and am always very happy while doing so, to the point that other people have commented about how upbeat I always am while firefighting.

This all brings me to my question(s) (finally right?). If Odin is communicating with me, I feel like it would be rude not to respond and the last thing I want to do is offend, but I'm not clear on how to respond. Do I make an offering? Do I set up an alter? Do I just somehow ask directly? Should I reach out to Baldr? Because I have never tried to communicate with any gods before and this is rather outside my comfort zone, I would really appreciate a little bit of guidance.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
This is a fairly common experience these days! Fortunately the gods are used to people being baffled by them, since we in the modern industrial world by and large are so lamentably ignorant about such simple and important matters.

The gods hear prayers. That's the first thing to keep in mind. You don't need to do the kneel-and-fold-your-hands thing -- that's specifically a Christian practice, not suited to the old gods. Stand on your own two feet, clear your mind, turn your attention to Odin, thank him for reaching out to you, and ask him for his help in figuring out what you should do. Since he's already pretty clearly communicating with you, you'll get an answer, either immediately or in some improbable way in the near future.
